title: Python SDK wrapping snarkjs for LangChain/CrewAI devs
description: Build a PyPI package `bolyra` that shells out to snarkjs via subprocess for proof generation/verification, exposing `create_identity()`, `enroll_agent()`, `prove_handshake()`, `verify_handshake()`, and `delegate()`. Include typed dataclasses for all inputs/outputs and async variants for non-blocking use in agent event loops. LangChain and CrewAI developers live in Python — without this, adoption requires JS interop that kills DX.

title: LangChain BolyraAuthTool with structured output
description: Implement a `langchain.tools.BaseTool` subclass that wraps the Python SDK to perform mutual auth before sensitive operations. The tool should accept a `HandshakeRequest` Pydantic model, return a `HandshakeResult` with proof validity, scope bitmask, and nullifier, and integrate with LangChain's tool-calling agent pattern. Include a `BolyraAuthCallback` that auto-injects handshake verification into any chain step tagged with `requires_auth=True`. This is the 3-line integration that makes LangChain devs actually try Bolyra.

title: CrewAI task delegation mapped to Bolyra scope narrowing
description: Create a CrewAI `Process` plugin that intercepts `Task.delegate()` calls and generates a Bolyra delegation proof with scope narrowing. Map CrewAI's role-based permissions (e.g., researcher can read but not write) to the 64-bit permission bitmask, enforcing that delegated agents cryptographically cannot exceed their delegator's scope. Emit delegation nullifiers as CrewAI task metadata so the entire delegation chain is auditable. This turns CrewAI's trust-based delegation into cryptographically enforced delegation.

title: AutoGen GroupChat mutual auth middleware
description: Build an AutoGen `ConversableAgent` mixin that performs a Bolyra handshake at the start of every GroupChat session and on agent join. Each agent proves its credential before participating; the GroupChatManager verifies all proofs and rejects agents with stale roots or revoked credentials. This prevents prompt-injection-based impersonation in multi-agent conversations — a real attack vector in AutoGen's open message-passing model.

title: Human-readable scope permission registry and bitmask builder
description: The raw 64-bit bitmask is unusable for framework developers. Build a `BolyraScopes` registry that maps named permissions (e.g., `SEARCH_WEB`, `EXECUTE_CODE`, `FINANCIAL_READ`, `FINANCIAL_WRITE_10K`) to bit positions, validates cumulative encoding invariants (bit 4 implies 2+3), and provides a fluent builder: `Scopes.allow('SEARCH_WEB', 'FINANCIAL_READ').to_bitmask()`. Ship as part of the Python SDK with a CLI tool `bolyra scopes describe 0x...` that prints human-readable permission sets. Without this, every integration will hardcode magic numbers.

title: Agent-to-agent marketplace handshake flow
description: The current protocol only models human-to-agent and delegation chains, but the 10-year trajectory for agentic commerce requires agent-to-agent service discovery and mutual authentication without a human in the loop. Add a new AgentHandshake circuit that proves two agents both hold valid credentials, their scopes overlap on the required capability, and neither is revoked — outputting a bilateral session token. This unlocks autonomous agent marketplaces where agents can transact services (e.g., an LLM agent hiring a code-review agent) with cryptographic accountability back to their respective operators.

title: Payment channel scope-bound authorization
description: Agentic commerce needs agents to spend money on behalf of humans with enforceable limits. Extend the Delegation circuit to output a paymentCapCommitment that binds a maximum spend amount (in wei or stablecoin units) to the delegatee's scope commitment. The on-chain contract tracks cumulative spend per delegation nullifier and reverts when the cap is exceeded. This turns Bolyra from an identity/auth protocol into a commerce protocol — agents can autonomously purchase services up to their delegated budget without per-transaction human approval.

title: Lightweight session resumption without re-proving
description: Current handshakes require a full ZK proof per session, which is a 5-15 second wall-clock cost that kills UX for interactive agent workflows. Add a session resumption mechanism: after a successful handshake, the contract emits a sessionId derived from Poseidon(humanNullifier, agentNullifier, nonce). Subsequent interactions within a TTL window (e.g., 1 hour) can reference the sessionId with a cheap ECDSA signature instead of regenerating proofs. This is the single biggest adoption lever — developers won't integrate a protocol that adds 10 seconds to every API call.

title: OpenAI function-calling native adapter
description: LangChain and CrewAI are important but the largest surface area for agentic commerce is OpenAI's function-calling API, which is used by the vast majority of production agent deployments. Build a @bolyra/openai adapter that wraps any OpenAI tool definition with automatic mutual auth: before the function executes, the adapter runs proveHandshake() and attaches the proof to the tool call context. Ship it as an npm package with a one-line wrapper: `bolyraProtect(toolDef, identity)`. Target the path of least resistance — the 500k+ developers already building on OpenAI's API.

title: Privacy-preserving delegation audit trail
description: For enterprise and regulated agentic commerce (credit unions, financial services), operators need to audit what their delegated agents did — without breaking the privacy of the humans they interacted with. Add an AuditProof circuit that takes a delegation chain and produces a verifiable summary: operator identity, scope used, timestamp range, and action count — but with the human's identity replaced by their nullifier. Store audit roots on-chain with a 90-day retention window. This satisfies SOC2 and FFIEC audit requirements while preserving Bolyra's privacy guarantees, making the protocol viable for regulated industries.

title: Cross-chain root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
description: Implement a RootRelay contract on Arbitrum and Polygon that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs of agentRootHistory and humanRootHistory slots from the Base IdentityRegistry. A relayer submits the block header (via the L1 state root accessible on each L2) plus the Merkle-Patricia proof of the storage slot. This avoids bridges and oracles entirely — verification is pure math against the L1 anchor. Estimated 3 contracts (RootRelay, StorageProofLib, ChainRegistry) plus a TypeScript relayer script.

title: Chain-agnostic nullifier registry with cross-chain replay protection
description: The current nullifierHash is Poseidon2(scope, secret) and is chain-unaware. If the same verifier contract is deployed on Base and Arbitrum, a proof verified on Base can be replayed on Arbitrum since nullifier sets are per-chain. Fix: include chainId in the nullifier derivation (Poseidon3(scope, secret, chainId)) and add a chainId public input to HumanUniqueness and AgentPolicy circuits. This is a breaking change to the circuit but critical before multi-chain deployment.

title: Multi-chain VerificationGateway with chain-local proof acceptance
description: Deploy a VerificationGateway contract on Arbitrum and Polygon that wraps the same PLONK/Groth16 verifier logic but accepts roots from RootRelay instead of local trees. The gateway exposes the same verifyHandshake() interface as IdentityRegistry so SDK consumers don't need chain-specific code. Include a chainId => gatewayAddress mapping in the TypeScript SDK so proveHandshake() auto-routes to the correct chain. This is the user-facing counterpart to the storage proof root sync.

title: Cross-chain conformance test vectors
description: Add 15+ JSON test vectors to the conformance suite covering multi-chain scenarios: valid proof on chain A with synced root from chain B, replay attempt across chains (must reject), stale cross-chain root (relay delay > 30 roots), chainId mismatch in nullifier, and gateway routing with mismatched verifier addresses. These vectors should be runnable against any chain's VerificationGateway deployment to certify interop compliance. Include a Hardhat test harness that forks Base/Arbitrum/Polygon.

title: Cross-chain delegation hop continuity
description: Currently the delegation chain (max 3 hops) must complete on a single chain because each hop references the previous scopeCommitment and delegateeMerkleRoot from local state. Enable a delegation that starts on Base (hop 0) to continue on Arbitrum (hop 1) by having the Delegation circuit accept a crossChainScopeAttestation public input — a storage proof that the previousScopeCommitment exists in the origin chain's DelegationVerified event log. This lets agents delegate across chains without restarting the chain.

title: Interoperability profile with MUST/SHOULD conformance levels
description: Define a formal interoperability profile document that partitions Bolyra protocol features into MUST (core handshake, nullifier, Merkle verification), SHOULD (delegation, root history), and MAY (cross-chain, behavioral sybil) tiers using RFC 2119 keywords. Each tier gets a conformance level label (Level 1/2/3) so implementors can declare partial compliance. This prevents fragmentation where two 'Bolyra-compatible' implementations can't actually interoperate because they chose different optional features.

title: did:bolyra DID method specification
description: Write a W3C DID method spec for did:bolyra:<network>:<identityCommitment>. Define the DID Document structure mapping: humanMerkleRoot as verification method, nullifierHash as authentication proof, scopeCommitment as capability delegation. Specify the resolution algorithm (query IdentityRegistry for enrollment status, check revocation by nullifier). Include a Verifiable Credential issuance flow where a successful handshake produces a VC with the session nonce as challenge and both nullifiers as evidence. This bridges Bolyra into the existing SSI ecosystem.

title: CBOR wire format with CDDL schema for proof payloads
description: Replace ad-hoc JSON proof serialization with a CBOR-encoded wire format defined by a CDDL (RFC 8610) schema. Define message types: EnrollmentRequest, HandshakeChallenge, HandshakeProof (containing Groth16 + PLONK proof bytes, public signals array, session nonce), DelegationToken, and VerificationResult. CBOR gives deterministic encoding (needed for signature verification), compact binary representation (proofs are large), and a machine-parseable schema that test suites can validate against. Include an ABNF grammar for the text-encoded fallback format.

title: Cross-implementation conformance test vectors
description: Produce 60+ JSON test vectors organized by circuit (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation) and by outcome (valid, invalid-stale-root, invalid-revoked, invalid-scope-superset, invalid-expired, invalid-nonce-reuse, invalid-phantom-delegatee). Each vector includes: input witness, expected public signals, expected verification result, and the specific error code from the contract. Vectors cover the root history buffer edge case (root at position 29 vs evicted root at position 30) and the cumulative bit encoding invariant (bit 4 without bits 2+3). These become the canonical interop test suite — any conforming implementation must pass all vectors.

title: NIST SP 800-63B authenticator assurance level mapping
description: Publish an informational document mapping Bolyra authentication factors to NIST AAL levels. HumanUniqueness (EdDSA key + Merkle membership) maps to AAL1 single-factor crypto authenticator; AgentPolicy (EdDSA signature + operator binding + Merkle inclusion) maps to AAL2 multi-factor with restricted authenticator; the combined handshake (human proof + agent proof + session nonce) constitutes AAL2 verifier-impersonation-resistant authentication. Document the gap to AAL3 (requires hardware-bound authenticator — addressable by the platform attestation circuit in the roadmap). This mapping is essential for credit union adoption where NCUA examiners expect NIST alignment.

title: Prove delegation expiry narrowing is sound
description: The Delegation circuit claims delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry but the constraint enforcement is not visible in the truncated source. Write a Certora-style property spec that proves: for all valid witnesses, delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry AND delegateeExpiry <= currentTimestamp is rejected. Additionally, verify that the comparison uses the range-checked 64-bit values (not raw field elements) to prevent wraparound where a huge field element passes the LessThan comparator. Deliver: a .spec file with 4 invariants and a test harness generating boundary witnesses (expiry = 2^64-1, expiry = 0, expiry = delegatorExpiry ± 1).

title: Prove nullifier uniqueness across all three circuits
description: HumanUniqueness uses Poseidon2(scope, secret), AgentPolicy uses a nullifier derived from credential fields + nonce, and Delegation has its own delegationNullifier. No formal argument exists that these three nullifier domains are collision-resistant across circuits. Write a formal property showing domain separation: prove that no valid witness for circuit A can produce a nullifierHash that equals a valid nullifierHash from circuit B, by demonstrating the Poseidon input arities and domain tags differ. Deliver: a symbolic analysis document with Halo2 MockProver test cases exercising cross-circuit nullifier values on shared inputs.

title: Verify cumulative bit encoding is enforced in-circuit
description: The Delegation circuit header documents that bit 4 implies bits 2+3 and bit 3 implies bit 2, but the actual constraint enforcement must be verified. Write a Halo2 MockProver test suite that attempts to create valid witnesses violating cumulative encoding (e.g., bit 4 set without bit 3) and asserts they fail. Additionally, write a formal invariant proving that any satisfying witness for Delegation has delegateeScope where the cumulative property holds. This prevents a delegatee from receiving a 'financial unlimited' scope without the intermediate tiers. Deliver: 12+ negative test vectors and a property spec.

title: Prove Merkle proof length cannot exceed tree depth
description: All three circuits use BinaryMerkleRoot(MAX_DEPTH) with a variable merkleProofLength input. If merkleProofLength > MAX_DEPTH or merkleProofLength = 0, the circuit should reject the witness, but BinaryMerkleRoot's behavior at boundary values needs formal verification. Write a property proving: (1) merkleProofLength must be in [1, MAX_DEPTH], (2) setting merkleProofLength > MAX_DEPTH cannot produce a root matching any on-chain root, and (3) unused sibling slots beyond merkleProofLength do not influence the computed root. Deliver: MockProver boundary tests for length = 0, MAX_DEPTH, MAX_DEPTH+1, and a circom-level assertion patch if the bounds are not enforced.

title: Prove root history buffer guarantees liveness window
description: IdentityRegistry maintains 30-slot circular buffers for both humanRootHistory and agentRootHistory. Write a Certora specification for the Solidity contract proving: (1) every root added via enrollment is queryable via rootExists for exactly 30 subsequent enrollments, (2) the 31st enrollment evicts exactly the oldest root, (3) no root outside the buffer can pass rootExists, and (4) the humanRootExists and agentRootExists mappings stay in sync with their respective arrays. This catches edge cases like index wraparound bugs or stale mapping entries after buffer overflow. Deliver: a .spec file targeting IdentityRegistry with 6 invariants and a Certora harness.

title: Replace Num2Bits range checks with PLONK lookup tables
description: AgentPolicy and Delegation each use multiple Num2Bits(64) decompositions for range checks (~128 constraints each, 384+ total per circuit). PLONK supports lookup arguments (plookup) which can verify range membership in ~6 constraints per value by checking against a precomputed table. Replace all Num2Bits(64) instances with a shared lookup-based RangeCheck64 template. This cuts range-check constraints by ~95% and also eliminates bit decomposition wires that bloat the witness.

title: Restructure AgentPolicy Poseidon(5) credential hash to tree of Poseidon(2)
description: AgentPolicy computes credentialCommitment via Poseidon(5) (t=6, 8 full rounds + 57 partial rounds = ~330 constraints). Restructuring as a Merkle-style tree of three Poseidon(2) calls (t=3, 8+56 rounds each, ~200 constraints each) totals ~600 constraints — worse naively. However, the real win is that Poseidon(2) is the same hash used in the Merkle path, enabling round-constant sharing and a single optimized template. Benchmark both approaches and ship whichever yields fewer total constraints when the compiler deduplicates shared structure. Deliver a PoseidonTree template with comparative constraint counts.

title: Replace EdDSA verification with efficient ECDSA or Schnorr stub for AgentPolicy
description: EdDSA verification in AgentPolicy costs ~30k constraints — over 60% of the circuit budget. The circom-ecdsa-circuits library by Personae implements secp256k1 ECDSA verification at ~18k constraints using precomputed tables and windowed scalar multiplication. Alternatively, a BabyJubjub Schnorr verification (single scalar mul + hash check) costs ~12k constraints. Since operator keys are internal to the protocol (not wallet keys), switching to Schnorr on BabyJubjub is feasible and would cut total AgentPolicy constraints from ~50k to ~32k, directly improving mobile proving time.

title: Precompute BabyPbk outside HumanUniqueness circuit, verify via in-circuit assertion
description: HumanUniqueness derives the public key from the secret inside the circuit using BabyPbk (scalar multiplication on BabyJubjub, ~2k constraints and the slowest witness-generation step). Since the prover already knows their public key, pass (Ax, Ay) as private inputs and verify Ax, Ay lie on the curve and secret * G == (Ax, Ay) using a cheaper in-circuit check: replace the full scalar mul with a Poseidon-based commitment to (secret, Ax, Ay) and a subgroup check. This trades ~800 constraints for a 3-5x witness generation speedup, critical for mobile/browser proving targets.

title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment with minimal constraint overhead
description: The current scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment) is vulnerable to brute-force since the 64-bit bitmask has at most 2^64 preimages (and realistically far fewer due to cumulative encoding constraints). Add a 254-bit blindingSalt as a third Poseidon input: Poseidon(bitmask, credCommitment, blindingSalt). This upgrades from Poseidon(2) to Poseidon(3), adding only ~50 constraints (one extra partial round), while making offline enumeration computationally infeasible. Update AgentPolicy output, Delegation chain linking, and the SDK's commitment computation.

title: Bind expiry into scope commitment to prevent delegation duration manipulation
description: The Delegation circuit computes scopeCommitment = Poseidon(delegatorCredCommitment, delegateeCredCommitment, scope) without binding expiryTimestamp. This means a downstream verifier consuming scopeCommitment cannot distinguish a 1-hour delegation from a 1-year delegation — an attacker who obtains a short-lived delegation token can present it as if it were long-lived. Fix: include delegateeExpiry in the Poseidon hash for scopeCommitment and update the contract's scope-chain validation to match.

title: Delegation nullifier prevents scope renewal without scope change
description: The delegation nullifier is Poseidon(delegatorSecret, scope). Because neither expiry nor nonce is included, a delegator who revokes and re-delegates the same scope to the same or different agent produces an identical nullifier, which the contract rejects as DelegationNullifierReused. This forces artificial scope mutations for legitimate renewals. Fix: compute nullifier as Poseidon(delegatorSecret, scope, sessionNonce) so each session produces a unique nullifier while retaining per-session replay protection.

title: Add chain ID domain separation to all circuit nullifiers
description: No circuit (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation) binds proofs to a specific chain. A valid proof generated for Chain A can be replayed on Chain B since nullifiers and scope commitments are chain-agnostic. Add a public input chainId to each circuit, include it in nullifier computation (e.g., Poseidon(secret, scope, chainId)), and validate chainId == block.chainid in the IdentityRegistry contract. Without this, any multi-chain deployment is vulnerable to cross-chain proof replay.

title: Re-verify expiry ordering in contract as defense-in-depth
description: The AgentPolicy circuit enforces currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp via LessThan(64), and the contract trusts this entirely. If the circuit's LessThan gadget has an edge-case bug (e.g., field overflow at boundary values), expired credentials pass verification. The contract should redundantly check that the public signal currentTimestamp (which it already receives) is less than block.timestamp + MAX_CLOCK_DRIFT and that expiryTimestamp > block.timestamp. This is a 10-line change in IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() that provides a critical safety net.

title: Prevent root history buffer starvation under burst enrollment
description: With ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE=30 and Groth16 proving taking ~15s, a burst of 30+ enrollments within one proving window evicts all roots a prover might reference, causing their valid proof to fail with StaleAgentRoot/StaleHumanRoot. This is a griefing vector: an attacker with enrollment access can DoS legitimate provers by enrolling 30 dummy identities. Fix: increase ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE to 100, add a minimum root TTL (e.g., reject _pushRoot if last push was <1 block ago), and emit an event with evicted root for off-chain monitoring.

title: Fluent delegation chain builder API
description: Add a chainable DelegationBuilder to @bolyra/sdk that reads like English: `Delegation.from(agentA).to(agentB).narrowScope(['read']).expiresIn('1h').prove()`. Currently developers must manually construct raw field inputs (delegatorScope bitmask, expiryTimestamp, credential commitments) and wire Merkle proofs themselves. The builder handles bitmask encoding, timestamp math, snapshot fetching, and proof generation behind a single fluent chain, cutting the delegation hello-world from ~40 lines to 5.

title: Python SDK with async snarkjs subprocess bridge
description: Implement the bolyra PyPI package specified in tier2_007 outline: a Node.js subprocess bridge (stdin/JSON/stdout) wrapped in Pydantic v2 models with both sync and async APIs. The target audience is LangChain/CrewAI developers who need `bolyra.prove_handshake()` and `bolyra.delegate()` in Python without touching Node directly. Ship with a bundled node_bridge/index.js, structured exceptions (BridgeError, CircuitNotFoundError, ProofVerificationError), and a `bolyra[langchain]` extras install that includes the BolyraAuthTool BaseTool wrapper.

title: Unified error taxonomy across SDK, contract, and circuit layers
description: Define a single error code namespace (BOLYRA-E001 through BOLYRA-E099) that maps across all three layers: Solidity custom errors, TypeScript SDK exceptions, and Python SDK exceptions. Currently IdentityRegistry.sol has descriptive custom errors but the SDK throws generic `new Error()` strings, making it impossible to programmatically handle failures like stale roots vs. revoked identities. Each error gets a code, human message, and suggested remediation. Ship as a shared `errors.json` schema consumed by codegen for all three languages.

title: EventEmitter lifecycle hooks for credential state changes
description: Add an observable credential manager to @bolyra/sdk that emits events on state transitions: `onRootRotated`, `onCredentialExpiring(threshold)`, `onNullifierUsed`, `onDelegationChainBroken`. Developers building long-running agent services currently have no way to react to tree updates that invalidate cached proofs — they discover failures only at verification time. The manager polls the on-chain root history buffer and emits typed events, letting integrators refresh proofs proactively instead of failing at handshake time.

title: npx create-bolyra scaffolding CLI
description: Build a `npx create-bolyra` CLI that scaffolds a working project in under 60 seconds. It prompts for use case (human-agent handshake, agent delegation, or full pipeline), generates a project with circuit artifacts, a local Hardhat node with pre-deployed contracts, and a runnable `index.ts` that completes an end-to-end proof. This eliminates the current onboarding cliff: developers must manually compile circuits, run trusted setup, deploy three contracts, and construct Merkle trees before they can generate their first proof.
